summary: A Muslim, Persianate "Gunpowder Empire" founded in 1526 by Babur after his victory over the Delhi Sultanate at the First Battle of Panipat, ruling most of the Indian subcontinent until the mid-19th century. Its rulers included Akbar the Great, who built an inclusive administrative and religious order around Din-i-Ilahi and the Ibadat Khana; Shah Jahan, who built the Taj Mahal and the Peacock Throne; and Aurangzeb, under whom the empire reached its greatest extent before a ruinous war against the Maratha Confederacy and Nader Shah's 1739 sack of Delhi set it on its final decline, ending with the exile of Bahadur Shah II after the 1857 Sepoy Mutiny.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: A Muslim, Persianate "Gunpowder Empire" that ruled most of the Indian subcontinent from the 16th to the mid-19th century, successively led by Babur, Humayun, Akbar the Great, Jahangir, Shah Jahan, and Aurangzeb. In giveaways it is most often identified through Shah Jahan's white marble mausoleum the Taj Mahal, built in Agra for his wife Mumtaz, and through the jewel-encrusted Peacock Throne later housed in Delhi's Red Fort.

clues.clue: Shah Jahan built the Taj Mahal, a white marble mausoleum in Agra, for his wife Mumtaz
examples: name this empire whose Shah Jahan built the Taj Mahal.
name this empire ruled by Akbar, and where Shah Jahan memorialized his wife Mumtaz with the Taj Mahal.

clues.clue: Ruled successively by Babur, Humayun, Akbar the Great, Jahangir, Shah Jahan, and Aurangzeb
examples: name this Muslim empire ruled by figures like Humayun and Akbar the Great.
Both Jahangir and Aurangzeb were rulers of this Indian empire founded by Babur.

clues.clue: The jewel-encrusted Peacock Throne, built during Shah Jahan's reign, was later kept in the Red Fort at Delhi
examples: name this empire which built the Peacock Throne during the reign of Shah Jahan.
The Red Fort was home to this empire's Peacock Throne.

clues.clue: A Muslim "Gunpowder Empire" that ruled most of the Indian subcontinent from the 16th to 19th centuries
examples: name this Gunpowder Empire ruled by Akbar the Great.
Name this empire founded by Babur which ruled most of the Indian subcontinent from the sixteenth to the nineteenth centuries.

works.name: Babur & Humayun: Founding and Early Turmoil (1526-1556)
works.description: The empire began in 1526 when Babur -- a Central Asian prince who claimed descent from both Genghis Khan and Tamerlane and had been driven out of the Ferghana Valley -- defeated the Delhi Sultanate's ruler Ibrahim Lodi at the First Battle of Panipat, initially ruling from Agra; he narrated his exile, his conquest of Kabul, and his victory over Lodi in the Chagatai-language memoir the Baburnama. His son and successor Humayun died after falling down the library stairs while carrying books and kneeling for the evening call to prayer, clearing the way for his own son Akbar to take the throne. A mosque named for Babur, the Babri Masjid, was later destroyed in 1992 riots.

clues.clue: Babur, exiled from the Ferghana Valley and claiming descent from Genghis Khan and Tamerlane, defeated Ibrahim Lodi at the First Battle of Panipat to found the empire
examples: The first ruler of this empire defeated Ibrahim Lodi at the First Battle of Panipat after being driven out of the Ferghana Valley.
The first Battle of Panipat saw Babur defeat the forces of Ibrahim Lodi, thus paving the way for Babur to found this empire that would also include the reign of his grandson, Akbar.

clues.clue: Babur narrated his exile, his conquest of Kabul, and his victory over Ibrahim Lodi in the Chagatai-language memoir the Baburnama
examples: The first ruler of this empire wrote a lengthy epic that discusses his exile from the Ferghana Valley, his conquest of Kabul, and his defeat of Ibrahim Lodi at Panipat.
name this empire whose first ruler wrote the Baburnama.

clues.clue: The empire's early capital was Agra
examples: Name this empire, that was initially ruled from Agra.

clues.clue: Humayun, Babur's son and second emperor, died after falling down his library stairs while carrying books and kneeling for the call to prayer (adhan), allowing his son Akbar to take the throne
examples: Humayun was an emperor of this empire. He fell down the stairs while trying to carry books and kneel for the call to prayer at the same time allowing his son Akbar to ascend to the throne.

clues.clue: A mosque named for Babur, the Babri Masjid, was destroyed in riots in 1992
examples: A mosque destroyed in riots in 1992 was named for a ruler of this empire.

works.name: Akbar the Great: Administration & Din-i-Ilahi (1556-1605)
works.description: Akbar ended the rival Suri dynasty, abolished the jizya tax on non-Muslims, and founded the interfaith Ibadat Khana ("House of Worship") at his capital Fatehpur Sikri, where debates among scholars of different faiths syncretized into his own religion, Din-i-Ilahi. His court included the "Nine Jewels," intellectuals such as the poet Faizi and the musician Tansen, and his administrative reforms -- mansab ranks (zat and sawar) entitling nobles to jagirdari revenue, and Raja Todar Mal's standardization of the bureaucracy -- were recorded by his court historian Abu'l Fazl, who built on the earlier biography of Babur and Humayun written by the princess Gulbadan Begum; the historian Badauni wrote more critically of his reforms. Fatehpur Sikri's surviving buildings include the Diwan-i-Khas audience hall and the sandstone Buland Darwaza ("Door of Victory"). Raja Man Singh's victory at the Battle of Haldighati extended Akbar's control into Orissa, and his reign also saw the conquest of the independent sultanate of Gujarat.

clues.clue: Ended the Suri dynasty, abolished the jizya tax, and founded the interfaith Ibadat Khana ("House of Worship") at Fatehpur Sikri, whose debates syncretized into the new faith of Din-i-Ilahi
examples: After ending the Suri dynasty, a ruler of this empire abolished the jizya and created the interfaith ibadatkhana, for which he was accused of starting the new faith of "Din-i Ilahi."
Another ruler of this empire created the House of Worship to house debates between scholars of all faiths, after which he created his own syncretic faith called Din-e lahi.

clues.clue: His court included the "Nine Jewels," intellectuals such as the poet Faizi and the musician Tansen
examples: Faizi and Tansen are included amongst the names of nine intellectuals employed in the court of this empire known as "jewels."
The Nine Jewels served in the court of a ruler of this empire who promoted the syncretic religion Din-i Ilahi.

clues.clue: His reign's administrative reforms included mansab ranks (zat and sawar) entitling nobles to jagirdari revenue, and Raja Todar Mal's standardization of the bureaucracy
examples: In this empire, a noble's mansab entitled him to jagirdari revenue.
Abu'l Fazl is the main source on Raja Todar Mal's standardization of this empire's bureaucracy.

clues.clue: Abu'l Fazl, a member of the Nine Jewels, chronicled his reign, building on the earlier biography of Babur and Humayun written by the princess Gulbadan Begum; the historian Badauni also wrote critically of his reforms and popularized a hadith condemning rule by women, boys, or eunuchs
examples: Gulbadan Begum chronicled the life of one ruler of this empire, while the reign of another of this empire's rulers was chronicled by Abu'l Fazl, a member of the "Nine Jewels" Court.
This empire's historian Abdul Qadir Badauni helped popularize a hadith which claimed that leadership from women, boys, or eunuchs was a sign of doom.

clues.clue: Munis Faruqui argued that succession wars among the empire's princes were actually a source of stability through coalition-building, while Irfan Habib studied the empire's "agrarian crisis"
examples: Munis Faruqui has argued that the norm of succession wars among this empire's princes was in fact its main source of stability, due to the princes' coalition building.
Irfan Habib studied this empire's "agrarian crisis."

clues.clue: At Fatehpur Sikri, the private audience hall (Diwan-i-Khas) contains four diagonal walkways and a central goblet-shaped pillar with serpentine brackets, and several chhatris top the red-sandstone "Door of Victory" (Buland Darwaza)
examples: The interior of a private audience hall from this empire contains four diagonal walkways and a central goblet-shaped pillar with serpentine brackets.
Several chhatris top this empire's "Door of Victory" constructed in an abandoned palace with red sandstone topped by marble decorations.

clues.clue: Raja Man Singh's victory at the Battle of Haldighati extended the empire's control into Orissa
examples: Orissans also had an opportunity to die at the Battle of Haldighati, which marked Raja Man Singh's conquest of the region on behalf of this dynasty.

clues.clue: The empire conquered Gujarat, ending its independence as a sultanate, on its way to controlling most of India
examples: Gujarat managed to stay an independent sultanate until it was conquered by this Islamic empire founded by Babur, which came to control most of India.

works.name: Jahangir & Nur Jahan (1605-1628)
works.description: Akbar's son Jahangir succeeded him; his empress Nur Jahan allegedly killed four tigers with six bullets while hunting with her husband and was the only Mughal woman to have coinage struck in her own name, a distinction dramatized in Indu Sundaresan's novel The Twentieth Wife. Jahangir's rebellious son, Prince Khurram (the future Shah Jahan), unsuccessfully revolted against Nur Jahan and took refuge with Karan Singh II.

clues.clue: His empress Nur Jahan allegedly killed four tigers with six bullets while hunting with her husband
examples: While on a hunt with her husband, an empress from this empire allegedly killed four tigers with six bullets.

clues.clue: Nur Jahan was the only Mughal woman to have coinage struck in her name, and is dramatized in Indu Sundaresan's novel The Twentieth Wife
examples: The only woman in this polity to have coinage struck in her name is depicted in the novel The Twentieth Wife.

clues.clue: Prince Khurram (the future Shah Jahan) unsuccessfully rebelled against Nur Jahan and took refuge with Karan Singh II
examples: After this empire's Prince Khurram unsuccessfully rebelled against that empress, he took refuge with Karan Singh II.

works.name: Shah Jahan: The Golden Age of Mughal Architecture (1628-1658)
works.description: Shah Jahan commissioned the Taj Mahal, a white marble mausoleum for his late wife Mumtaz whose charbagh garden is bisected by a rectangular reflecting pool; it was designed by Ustad Ahmad Lahori with four minarets surrounding a white onion dome, and Shah Jahan apocryphally planned a "black" mirror version of it for his own tomb. His reign also saw pietra dura decoration applied to the "jewel box" Tomb of I'timad-ud-Daulah, nicknamed a "baby" version of the Taj Mahal, and the addition of an upper arcade to the deep Chand Baori stepwell.

clues.clue: The Taj Mahal, containing a charbagh bisected by a rectangular pool, was designed by Ustad Ahmad Lahori with four minarets surrounding a white onion dome; Shah Jahan apocryphally planned a "black" mirror version of it for himself
examples: A ruler of this empire apocryphally planned a "black" version of a building that contains a charbagh bisected by a rectangular pool.
That building from this empire designed by Ustad Ahmad Lahori has four minarets surrounding a white onion dome.

clues.clue: Pietra dura decorates the Tomb of I'timad-ud-Daulah, a "jewel box" building nicknamed a "baby" version of the Taj Mahal
examples: Pietra dura was used in a "jewel box" building named after this empire's "pillar of the state" often called a "baby" version of a later building from this empire.

clues.clue: Rulers of the empire added the upper arcade to the deep Chand Baori stepwell
examples: Rulers of this empire added the upper arcade to the very deep Chand Baori stepwell. Shah Jahan, a ruler of this empire, built the Taj Mahal.

works.name: Aurangzeb: Orthodoxy and Maximum Extent (1658-1707)
works.description: Aurangzeb was crowned after defeating his brothers at the Battle of Samugarh and imprisoning their father Shah Jahan in Agra Fort. Under him the empire reached its greatest territorial extent through the conquest of the Deccan sultanates of Bijapur and Golconda, though his religious fervor is sometimes blamed for catalyzing the empire's later decline. His rival Shivaji, founder of the Maratha confederacy, reportedly escaped Mughal custody by hiding in wicker baskets covered in flowers.

clues.clue: Aurangzeb was crowned after defeating his brothers at the Battle of Samugarh and imprisoning their father Shah Jahan in Agra Fort
examples: Shah Jahan was put under house arrest in Agra Fort by this future Mughal Emperor. This Mughal emperor was crowned after the battle of Samugarh.

clues.clue: Under Aurangzeb the empire reached its greatest extent in the south through the conquest of the Deccan sultanates of Bijapur and Golconda
examples: This dynasty's power in southern India reached its zenith under Aurangzeb, who led the conquest of Bijapur and Golconda.

clues.clue: The empire's decline is sometimes attributed to Aurangzeb's religious fervor
examples: This empire's decline may have been catalyzed by the religious fervor of Aurangzeb.

clues.clue: Shivaji, founder of the rival Maratha confederacy, reportedly escaped Mughal custody by hiding in wicker baskets covered in flowers
examples: A man managed to escape from a ruler of this empire by hiding in wicker baskets covered in flowers.
Shivaji repulsed attacks by this empire and founded its nemesis, the Maratha confederacy.

works.name: Sufi Philosophy & Intellectual Culture
works.description: Beyond Akbar's Din-i-Ilahi and Ibadat Khana debates, which unsettled orthodox clerics, the empire's court fostered rich philosophical exchange. Dara Shikoh, Shah Jahan's eldest son, oversaw a Persian translation of the Yoga Vasistha (the Jug-Basisht) while traveling with the Venetian adventurer Nicolao Manucci and wrote the Mingling of the Two Oceans; he and his father Shah Jahan were influenced by the Qadri Sufi order. His orthodox Naqshbandi rival Ahmad Sirhindi countered with the doctrine of wahdat al-shuhud, declaring "do not say 'hama ust,' say 'hama azust.'" Elsewhere, a Mughal ruler had his foreign minister commission translations of Descartes and Gassendi, and the French traveler Francois Bernier described a court ideologue's proto-social-contract theory undergirding the policy of sulh-i-kul ("peace with all").

clues.clue: Clerics were disturbed by interfaith debates a ruler held at the Ibadat Khana
examples: Clerics were disturbed by debates that a ruler of this empire held in the ʿIbadat Khana.

clues.clue: Dara Shikoh oversaw the Persian translation Jug-Basisht (Yoga Vasistha) while traveling with the Venetian Nicolao Manucci; the Qadri Sufi order influenced him and his father Shah Jahan
examples: The translation Jug-Basisht was overseen by a thinker from this empire who traveled with Nicolao Manucci.
The Qadri sect influenced Muslim rulers of this empire like Dara Shikoh, whose father Shah Jahan also ruled it.

clues.clue: Ahmad Sirhindi declared "do not say hama ust, say hama azust" in a key theological debate, founding a Naqshbandi lineage of conservative critics of colonial rule
examples: A thinker in this empire created one side in a key debate by declaring "do not say 'hama ust', say 'hama azust'" and founded a lineage that contained many conservative critics of colonial rule by redirecting the energy of the ʾAlfi movements.

clues.clue: A ruler had his foreign minister commission translations of Descartes and Gassendi from a student of Gassendi
examples: A ruler of this empire had his foreign minister commission translations of Descartes and Gassendi from a student of the latter.

clues.clue: The chief ideologue visited by Francois Bernier articulated a proto-social-contract theory to justify the sovereign's absolute power in defense of the sulh-i-kul policy of universal tolerance
examples: Contemporaneous with European social contract theorists, the chief ideologue of this empire visited by the Francois Bernier posited that people give a sovereign absolute power to protect property and end fights over social and cultural differences to defend the sulh-i kul policy.

clues.clue: Ahmad Sirhindi and Dara Shikoh (author of the Mingling of the Two Oceans), along with Abu'l Fazl, all wrote under this empire
examples: Ahmad Sirhindi wrote under this empire, as did the author of Mingling of the Two Oceans and Abu'l Fazl ibn Mubarak.

works.name: The Mughal-Maratha Wars (1680s-1730s)
works.description: From the 1680s the empire fought a prolonged, ultimately ruinous conflict against the rising Maratha Confederacy, beginning when the Mughals invaded the Deccan sultanate of Bijapur. The war dragged on for twenty-seven years, including an eight-year siege that finally captured the Maratha stronghold of Jinji Fort, and continued under the regency of Queen Tarabai until Aurangzeb's own death in 1707. The war's cost accelerated the empire's disintegration -- Delhi itself was eventually conquered by the Maratha Empire -- and it later suffered raids from the Pindari irregulars before its final collapse.

clues.clue: A twenty-seven-year war against the Marathas, in its final phase led by Queen Tarabai, included the empire's longest siege (eight years), which captured Jinji Fort
examples: This empire fought a twenty-seven year long conflict against a rival power that was led in the final phase of the conflict by Queen Tarabai.
The longest siege by this empire's army lasted eight years and led to the capture of Jinji Fort.

clues.clue: Tarabai continued the Marathas' war against Aurangzeb until his death in 1707
examples: Tarabai spent her regency prosecuting a war against this empire, ruled at the time by Aurangzeb.
Tarabai continued Shivaji's wars against this empire's ruler Aurangzeb until his death in 1707.

clues.clue: The war began when the Mughals invaded Bijapur, and ended when they crossed the Narmada River to defeat the Marathas, who then fractured into smaller states
examples: What two empires fought a bloody war that began when one side invaded Bijapur? Near the end of the war, one side crossed the Narmada River to defeat the other, which began to fracture into smaller states.

clues.clue: Delhi, the empire's capital, was eventually conquered by the Maratha Empire, and the empire began disintegrating after losses to the Marathas in the Deccan War
examples: Name this empire with a capital at Delhi which was conquered by the Maratha empire.
This empire began disintegrating after losing to the Maratha Empire in the Deccan War.

clues.clue: Pindari raiders launched raids into the empire before its collapse
examples: The Pindaris launched raids into this Muslim empire before its collapse. Rulers of this empire included Shah Jahan, the builder of the Taj Mahal.

works.name: Nader Shah's Invasion & the Fall of the Empire (1739-1857)
works.description: In 1739 the Afsharid ruler Nader Shah invaded Delhi, defeating the Mughals (then led by Muhammad Shah) at the Battle of Karnal and looting the capital, seizing the Peacock Throne, an artifact famously described by Jean-Baptiste Tavernier. The sack accelerated the empire's terminal decline, which ended over a century later when its last emperor, Bahadur Shah II, was exiled to Burma following the outbreak of the Sepoy Mutiny of 1857.

clues.clue: Nader Shah, an Afsharid ruler, raided the Red Fort and stole the Peacock Throne, an artifact famously described by Jean-Baptiste Tavernier, after defeating the Mughals at the Battle of Karnal
examples: The Afsharid Emperor Nader Shah raided this empire's Red Fort and stole the Peacock Throne.
Jean-Baptiste Tavernier gave a notable description of an artifact created by this empire, which was dealt a major defeat at the Battle of Karnal by an Afsharid ruler.

clues.clue: At the time of Nader Shah's conquest, Delhi was ruled by this gunpowder empire, then under Muhammad Shah
examples: Delhi, at the time of Nader Shah's conquest, was ruled by this gunpowder empire centered in India.

clues.clue: The empire's last emperor, Bahadur Shah II, was exiled to Burma after the outbreak of the Sepoy Mutiny
examples: Name this empire, whose last leader, Bahadur Shah II, was exiled to Burma after the outbreak of the Sepoy mutiny.

comprehensive_summary: The Mughal Empire began in 1526 when Babur, a Central Asian prince claiming descent from both Genghis Khan and Tamerlane who had been driven from the Ferghana Valley, defeated the Delhi Sultanate's Ibrahim Lodi at the First Battle of Panipat and initially ruled from Agra, narrating his conquests (including the taking of Kabul) in the Chagatai-language memoir the Baburnama. His son Humayun died after falling down his library stairs while carrying books and kneeling for the evening call to prayer, opening the way for Akbar the Great, who ended the rival Suri dynasty, abolished the jizya tax, and founded the interfaith Ibadat Khana at his capital Fatehpur Sikri, whose debates syncretized into his own religion, Din-i-Ilahi; his court included the "Nine Jewels" (among them the poet Faizi and musician Tansen), and his administrative reforms -- mansab ranks entitling nobles to jagirdari revenue, and Raja Todar Mal's standardization of the bureaucracy -- were recorded by the court historian Abu'l Fazl, building on Gulbadan Begum's earlier chronicle, while Badauni wrote more critically; Akbar also conquered Gujarat and, through Raja Man Singh's victory at Haldighati, extended control into Orissa. Akbar's son Jahangir was succeeded in turn by his own son, Shah Jahan, though not before Jahangir's empress Nur Jahan -- the only Mughal woman to have coinage struck in her name, and the reputed killer of four tigers with six bullets on a hunt -- saw Jahangir's rebellious son Prince Khurram take refuge with Karan Singh II after an unsuccessful revolt. As Shah Jahan, Khurram commissioned the Taj Mahal, a white marble mausoleum for his late wife Mumtaz designed by Ustad Ahmad Lahori with four minarets around a white onion dome, and apocryphally planned a "black" mirror version of it for his own tomb; his reign also produced the pietra-dura-decorated Tomb of I'timad-ud-Daulah (a "baby" Taj Mahal) and an added arcade atop the Chand Baori stepwell. Shah Jahan's son Aurangzeb seized the throne after defeating his brothers at the Battle of Samugarh and imprisoning his father in Agra Fort, expanding the empire to its greatest extent through conquest of the Deccan sultanates of Bijapur and Golconda, though his religious fervor is sometimes blamed for the empire's subsequent decline; his rival Shivaji, founder of the Maratha confederacy, reportedly escaped Mughal custody hidden in flower-covered baskets. Beyond statecraft, the court fostered deep philosophical exchange: Shah Jahan's son Dara Shikoh, influenced like his father by the Qadri Sufi order, translated the Yoga Vasistha (as the Jug-Basisht) while traveling with the Venetian Nicolao Manucci and wrote the Mingling of the Two Oceans, opposed by the orthodox Naqshbandi thinker Ahmad Sirhindi's doctrine of wahdat al-shuhud ("do not say 'hama ust,' say 'hama azust'"); elsewhere a Mughal ruler commissioned translations of Descartes and Gassendi, and the French traveler Francois Bernier described a court ideologue's proto-social-contract justification for the sulh-i-kul policy of universal tolerance. From the 1680s the empire fought a ruinous twenty-seven-year war against the Marathas that began with a Mughal invasion of Bijapur and included an eight-year siege that captured Jinji Fort; the war continued under the regency of Queen Tarabai until Aurangzeb's death in 1707, and its cost -- compounded by the eventual Maratha conquest of Delhi and later Pindari raids -- accelerated the empire's disintegration. In 1739 the Afsharid ruler Nader Shah dealt the empire (then under Muhammad Shah) a crushing defeat at the Battle of Karnal, sacking Delhi and carrying off the jewel-encrusted Peacock Throne, an object famously described by Jean-Baptiste Tavernier. The empire limped on in reduced form for over a century until its last emperor, Bahadur Shah II, was exiled to Burma following the outbreak of the Sepoy Mutiny in 1857.
recursive_suggestions: Akbar the Great -- dense administrative/religious clue cluster (Din-i-Ilahi, Nine Jewels, Fatehpur Sikri); could support a standalone figure page
Shah Jahan -- dense architecture cluster (Taj Mahal, capital move); appeared as its own answerline (excluded here) and could stand alone
Aurangzeb -- dense Deccan-conquest/Maratha-war cluster; appeared as its own answerline (excluded here) and could stand alone
Dara Shikoh -- Sufi-Vedic philosopher prince with his own cluster (Mingling of the Two Oceans / Majma-ul-Bahrain, Jug-Basisht)
Taj Mahal / Fatehpur Sikri architecture -- dense Fine Arts cluster on Mughal miniature painting and monuments; consider a standalone VFA topic
Nader Shah / Afsharid dynasty -- recurring invader, appeared partly as its own answerline in adjacent bonus parts
Timurid Dynasty -- the Mughals' claimed ancestral dynasty; appeared as its own answerline (T15 in the fetched set) and was excluded from this page
Maratha Empire/Confederacy -- recurring rival power; appeared as its own answerline in multiple bonuses
Sher Shah Suri / Sur dynasty -- interrupted Mughal rule under Humayun; appeared as its own answerline and was excluded

Ambiguity flag: "Mughal emperor(s)" (the title) is a separate, densely-clued Fine Arts answerline about Mughal miniature-painting conventions (hourglass/cherub/lion iconography) -- excluded from this page; consider a standalone VFA topic on Mughal painting

Ambiguity flag: "Mughal forts," "India," "Urdu," and "the Kingdom of Nepal" also surfaced as distinct answerlines from this search and were excluded -- filter to History + Mughal Empire specifically when re-querying
